metadata.evaluator_organization: AGH University of Science and Technology

evaluation_inputs.system_description: The Dynamic Map is a complex information system designed for urban traffic optimization. It consists of spatial databases storing static and dynamic data (road infrastructure, traffic conditions, events) and software modules for data collection from various sensors (video, acoustic, GPS), interpretation, and provision to clients (visualization, route planning, traffic control).

phase_1_discovery.initial_documentation_status: Generic architectural views (ArchiMate) were available but lacked constructs to describe scenario influence on components or capture specific design decisions. Some specifications were missing or decisions had not yet been made.
evaluator_inquiry_questions: What is the platform for this component (e.g., PostgreSQL, Linux, IIS)?
Is the component logic fixed or customizable?
What is the query granularity (bulk, low, medium, high)?
What communication type is used (synchronous, asynchronous)?

hardware_view_description: Data originates from a network of fixed sensors (video detectors, acoustic sensors, inductive loops)
Data includes on-board GPS receivers installed in vehicles
End-user interaction via network or mobile interfaces
runtime_constraints: Real-time performance requirements for traffic analysis
High volume of data feeds from sensors
Interoperability with various sensor types and client modules

questions: How are transactions distributed across the distinct repositories (Static, Traffic, Event)?
How are ontology definitions synchronized with database dictionaries?
Is the query granularity optimized for bulk updates vs. individual reads?

raw_candidate_scenarios.description: New sensor types and sensor processing software will be integrated in <6 days>
Dynamic Map will be capable of accepting feeds from up to 1000 sensors occurring every <60> sec
Dynamic Map will be capable of processing up to <200> read queries per second
New map features can be introduced and made discoverable with built-in functionality
Updating of data defining the road network can cause a loss of at most 10 values of a single monitoring parameter
Sensor failures will be detected in a configurable period ranging from 3 to 20 minutes
New event types and corresponding customizable detection routines can be introduced in 1 day
System will be able to accommodate and interpret location data from 5000 GPS tracker units updated every 5 sec
A Dynamic Map component can be ported to another platform within 20 days

business_goals: Optimize road traffic and provide tools for efficient traffic control
Enable fast response to road accidents or threats using real-time analysis
Provide efficient route planning and optimization based on dynamic traffic data
Integrate existing and new sensor infrastructure seamlessly
technical_constraints: Need for interoperability with diverse subsystems
architectural_drivers: Modifiability (Integration of new sensors and event types)

architectural_approaches.description: The system is divided into three main repositories: Static Map, Traffic Repository, and Event Repository. Each has its own database and interface facades.

addressed_attributes.rationale: Separation of concerns allows independent evolution of static data models and dynamic traffic data.
Allows optimization of storage and query strategies specific to the data type (static vs. high-velocity dynamic data).

architectural_approaches.description: Data structures and dictionaries for monitoring parameters and events are defined using ontologies.

addressed_attributes.rationale: Provides a semantic reference that supports seamless integration and extension of data types.

architectural_approaches.description: Access to databases and internal logic is wrapped by Facade components (e.g., IMS Facade, Feed Facade) exposing Web Service interfaces.

addressed_attributes.rationale: Standardizes communication and hides implementation details from diverse clients and sensors.

scenario_reference.text: New sensor types and sensor processing software will be integrated in <6 days>

analysis_records.stimulus: Requirement to add a new sensor type (e.g., videodetector)
architectural_decisions: Use of ontologies for structure definition
Discoverable directory in Traffic Repository (TM) database
Web Service interface for data feeds
Fixed structure for TM database directory
analysis_records.reasoning: Adding a sensor requires updating the ontology and the database dictionaries. The Traffic Repository couples directory and storage functions. While the ontology supports flexibility, the fixed database structure (chosen for performance) hinders changes. There is no automated workflow to propagate ontology changes to the database dictionaries.

risks.description: Risk of losing cohesion between ontology and database dictionaries.
risks.rationale: No decision or workflow exists for updating the database dictionaries based on ontology changes.

non_risks.description: Data structures and interfaces support seamless integration.
non_risks.rationale: The design explicitly accounts for diverse sensor types via generalized interfaces.

sensitivity_points.parameter: Location of type definitions (Ontology vs. Database)

tradeoff_points.parameter: Coupling of directory and storage in Traffic Repository

scenario_reference.text: Dynamic Map will be capable of accepting feeds from up to 1000 sensors occurring every <60> sec

analysis_records.reasoning: Sensors send data via synchronous web services to a buffer. A background process performs bulk inserts of up to 20,000 values every 5 seconds. Tests show this handles the load within the 10-second latency limit.

non_risks.description: Database performance is sufficient for the load.
non_risks.rationale: Initial tests confirm latency stays within limits using the bulk insert strategy.
